steps.title: State the logical relationship between the sentences
steps.content: Relationship: cause-effect. Sentence 1 states the cause: residents rely on the Cairo Metro for millions of trips each year (high demand). Sentence 2 states the effect: city officials work hard to maintain the system's stations. The officials' effort is a direct response to that heavy ridership.

steps.title: Match the relationship to the transition — per-choice diagnosis
steps.content:
• Choice A — "For this reason,": signals that sentence 2 is the result of what was described in sentence 1. High ridership (cause) explains why officials strive to maintain the stations (effect). Correct.
  • Choice B — "However,": signals contrast. There is nothing contradictory between high ridership and officials maintaining stations — they point in the same direction. Eliminated.
  • Choice C — "For example,": signals that sentence 2 provides a specific instance of a general claim. But sentence 2 is not an example of millions of trips; it is a consequence of them. Eliminated.
  • Choice D — "Alternatively,": signals a different option or substitute. Maintenance is not an alternative to heavy ridership; it is a response to it. Eliminated.
